item.title: Wait for Black Friday to build this unbeatable $1,500 PC
item.itunes:title: Wait for Black Friday to build this unbeatable $1,500 PC
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Building a new gaming PC is always exciting, especially during a sales event. With Black Friday almost upon us (many deals are already live), it makes sense to wait a few more weeks for the best PC component deals to arrive. If you've been saving up for a premium mid-range gaming PC, we've put together arguably the most overpowered gaming-focused build in a budget of $1,500. Black Friday would be the best time to shop for this build as you might even find this configuration for much less than $1,500.

  • Source: AMD
    AMD Ryzen 5 7600
    Best CPU
    $229 at Amazon
  • Source: Thermalright
    Thermalright Peerless Assassin 120 SE ARGB
    Best CPU cooler
    $35 at Amazon
  • Source: Zotac
    ZOTAC Gaming GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Trinity OC
    Best GPU
    $785 at Amazon
  • Source: Newegg
    ASRock B650M Pro RS WiFi
    Best B650 motherboard
    $125 at Newegg
  • Source: Adata
    ADATA XPG Lancer DDR5 RAM
    Best RAM
    $100 at Newegg
  • Samsung 980 Pro 2TB
    Best SSD
    $120 at Amazon
  • MSI MAG A850GL
    Best PSU
    $110 at Amazon
  • Corsair 4000X
    Best case
    $120 at Amazon

Unbeatable $1,500 gaming PC build for Black Friday

Source: AMD
AMD Ryzen 5 7600
Best CPU

You won't miss the 7600X

Despite more powerful AM5 chips available, the Ryzen 5 7600 is all you need for a high-end 1440p Ultra gaming experience. This 6-core powerhouse is capable of anything you throw at it, and its 65W TDP further keeps things cool and calm — without compromising performance.

$229 at Amazon $229 at Best Buy $229 at Newegg

AMD's new AM5 platform might not have had a stellar debut as far as value was concerned, but things have more than settled down now. AM5 motherboards, DDR5 RAM, and even Ryzen 7000 CPUs have mostly come down in price significantly. The Ryzen 5 7600 might be selling for its $229 MSRP right now, but you'll most probably see a price cut when Black Friday is in full swing. This 6-core, 12-thread CPU is essentially a Ryzen 5 7600X with slightly lower clocks and a lower TDP.

In terms of gaming performance, this translates to an excellent 1440p experience nonetheless, with its 5.1GHz boost clock enough to go toe-to-toe with your graphics card (which I'll come to in a moment). Its 65W TDP will even let you get by with a barebones cooler if you don't want to buy a new one, although I highly recommend you do. Overall, if you want an upgradable platform that lets you drop in one or two CPU upgrades down the line, the Ryzen 5 7600 is one of the best gaming CPUs to base your build around.

Source: Thermalright
Thermalright Peerless Assassin 120 SE ARGB
Best CPU cooler

Incredible cooling on a budget

The Peerless Assassin 120 SE has been on our list of the best CPU coolers for good reason. It's highly capable, looks great, is more than enough for an overclocked Ryzen 5 7600. And the best part — it costs less than $40.

$35 at Amazon $35 at Newegg

While the Ryzen 5 7600 might not be a particularly power-hungry chip, there's still a lot of value to be had from a decent air cooler. And the Thermalright Peerless Assassin 120 SE is one of the best air coolers you can get for under $40. The dual-tower cooler comes with two 120mm fans capable of running at 1,500 RPM and generating 66 CFM of airflow. It's even rated for 265W, so you should have no complaints even with an overclocked Ryzen 5 7600.

The Peerless Assassin SE 120 looks impressive with its sleek black and chrome design and dual RGB fans, so you'll not miss a liquid AIO — either for performance or looks. Going with an air cooler for this build is also crucial to maximizing the value by spending elsewhere, especially when the CPU I've picked doesn't need a liquid cooler. You can still enjoy a silent and cool operation with this stellar air cooler.

Source: Zotac
ZOTAC Gaming GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Trinity OC
Best GPU

Enjoy the latest titles at Ultra settings

$785 $880 Save $95

The RTX 4070 Ti Trinity OC from Zotac is a monster graphics card in a $1,500 PC. You're getting a significant performance uplift compared to the RTX 4070 and a design that's simply sensational. It's capable of 1440p high-end and even decent 4K gaming.

$785 at Amazon $785 at Newegg

The graphics card I've picked for this unbeatable $1,500 gaming build is the Zotac Gaming GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Trinity OC. Not only is this one of the best RTX 4070 Ti graphics cards on the market, it offers incredible gaming performance for the price. Sitting between the RTX 4070 and RTX 4080, it enjoys a unique position — packing high-end GPU power without costing as much as an entire PC. Fun fact — the Zotac Trinity OC is also one of the only graphics cards for Cyberpunk 2077's path tracing mode. It's very likely to see a further price cut during Black Friday, so waiting is definitely worth it.

In addition to handling edge cases like Cyberpunk 2077 maxed out at 1440p (thanks to Frame Generation, DLSS 3.5, and Ray Reconstruction), the RTX 4070 Ti Trinity OC can even breach 100 FPS at 4K Ultra settings in many modern titles such as Watch Dogs: Legion, Forza Horizon 5, and Assassin's Creed Valhalla. You can pick an AMD GPU like the RX 7900 XT or even the older RX 6950 XT to reduce your overall budget, but I'd rather pick a solid ray tracing GPU when building a $1,500 budget.

Source: Newegg
ASRock B650M Pro RS WiFi
Best B650 motherboard

Great value and reliability

$125 $150 Save $25

The ASRock B650M Pro RS WiFi is one of the most well-rounded B650 motherboards you can buy. Despite its lower price and micro-ATX form factor, you're getting everything you need for a killer Ryzen 7000 build.

$125 at Newegg $125 at Walmart

For the motherboard, you don't need anything more than a decent B650 option. And while the ASRock B650M Pro RS WiFi is not one of the best AM5 motherboards, it certainly comes close. To maximize the value from this build and squeeze in that RTX 4070 Ti, I picked this micro-ATX option from ASRock that packs in all the necessary features you need. It even looks great and is bound to be in sync with the overall theme.

For a spectacular price of $125 (which is likely to remain through Black Friday), you're getting three M.2 slots, including a PCIe 5.0 slot. An 8-phase power design, Wi-Fi 6E, and Bluetooth 5.2 offer a competitive value proposition compared to many other boards. You're even getting a USB 3.2 Gen2 Type-C port and some RGB as well. The ASRock B650M Pro RS WiFi is known to provide a stable experience even in intense scenarios, making this one of the best deals you can get.

Source: Adata
ADATA XPG Lancer DDR5 RAM
Best RAM

Hits right in the Ryzen sweet spot

$105 $120 Save $15

The XPG Lancer RGB DDR5 6000 CL30 kit offers unbelievable performance and aesthetics for just around $100. You're getting 32GB of 6000MT/s CL30 RAM, which happens to be the sweet spot for the AM5 Ryzen processors.

$105 at Amazon $100 at Newegg

I believe that the graphics card, storage, PSU, and RAM are the components you shouldn't skimp on, especially on a $1,500 budget. In that vein, I've picked the Adata XPG Lancer RGB DDR5 32GB kit from for this build. The combo of 6000MT/s and CL30 is the sweet spot where you don't sacrifice performance too much and stop yourself from overspending as well. The kit comes AMD EXPO certified so you'll not face any stability issues either.

As for its looks, the kit offers a sleek black design with tasteful RGB. There's also a white option but the former looks just right for this build. Compared to many other kits from Corsair or G.Skill, this Adata kit provides arguably the best balance between price, performance, and design, making this one of the best DDR5 RAM on the market.

Samsung 980 Pro 2TB
Best SSD

Still one of the fastest SSDs

For any gaming build, 1TB might be just fine, but the wildly popular Samsung 980 Pro 2TB NVMe is currently retailing at nearly its all-time lowest price, and is an easy recommendation. It's an ultra-fast Gen4 SSD with 2GB DRAM and a 5-year warranty.

$120 at Amazon $130 at Newegg

When it comes to the best SSDs for gaming, the Samsung 980 Pro might have been superseded by its successor, the 990 Pro, but from a value-for-money standpoint, the 980 Pro is still a flagship-grade SSD. It comes with Samsung's best-in-class Gen4 performance, pleasing design, and hardware-based encryption. This is a DRAM SSD from one of the top players in the space and currently costs barely more than $100.

You might spot even better deals for the 980 Pro on Black Friday, making the overall package even sweeter. For a gaming SSD, it's hard to find a faster and more reliable option at this price. And even in your non-gaming workloads, its excellent sustained write performance will let you breeze through anything you throw at it.

MSI MAG A850GL
Best PSU

Excellent ATX 3.0 power for cheap

$110 $140 Save $30

MSI's MSG A850GL is an incredibly competitive ATX 3.0 and PCIe 5.0 PSU to power your future-ready Ryzen build. With a native 12VHPWR connector, rock-solid components, and 7-year warranty, this PSU is not kidding around.

$110 at Amazon $120 at Newegg

The latest ATX 3.0 power supplies started out being extremely expensive but have now settled down. To power this particular build, the MSI MAG A850GL is the perfect choice for more reasons than one. First, it's well-reviewed and extremely popular in the community, thanks to its quality construction, fully modular design, and future-ready features. Second, even though you don't absolutely need it in this build, the 850W of power sets you up comfortably for future GPU or CPU upgrades, And, third, it's priced extremely well.

For little more than $100 (which happens to be its lowest price ever on Amazon), you're ensuring clean and reliable power for your precious components, plus the native 12VHPWR connector translates to a hassle-free connection to your RTX 40-series graphics card. Come Black Friday, there might not be further reduction in this PSU's price, but you might just be able to snag the current deal even then.

Corsair 4000X
Best case

Compact yet powerful, great-looking PC case

$120 $145 Save $25

The Corsair 4000X RGB case might not be one of the cheapest out there, but its quality materials and design, plenty of airflow, built-in RGB fans, and easy cable management make it one of the best ones on the market.

$120 at Amazon $120 at Newegg

To end with, I picked the well-known Corsair 4000X RGB case to build this gaming PC in. It might not feature on our list of the best PC cases for gamers, but that doesn't mean it's not a great case. First off, you're getting a great-looking design with a side and front tempered glass to show off your build. Second, there's plenty of room and cable management features to make your build process smoother. Third, you're getting impressive airflow and hence, performance. And lastly, there are three RGB fans at the front already.

There are even more quality-of-life features in this excellent case. The side panels slide off easily, making the process tool-less. There's a screw mount for you to put in a vertical GPU mount if you prefer. And you can easily sync the RGB with Corsair's iCUE software. One of the only downsides of this case might be the lack of 360mm radiator support at the top (you can put a 280mm one). But the rest of the package is irresistible, especially at the current price.

Hold your breath till Black Friday

At the current prices, this build might not look like a $1,500 rig. But you can shave off around $100 once Black Friday deals are live. The CPU and GPU chosen will most likely see a price reduction, while the PSU and case are strong maybes. If you're not one for waiting, you can also check out the best gaming pre-builds for some fantastic options.

Unbeatable $1,500 gaming PC build for Black Friday

AMD Ryzen 5 7600
Best CPU

You won't miss the 7600X

Despite more powerful AM5 chips available, the Ryzen 5 7600 is all you need for a high-end 1440p Ultra gaming experience. This 6-core powerhouse is capable of anything you throw at it, and its 65W TDP further keeps things cool and calm — without compromising performance.

$229 at Amazon $229 at Best Buy $229 at Newegg

AMD's new AM5 platform might not have had a stellar debut as far as value was concerned, but things have more than settled down now. AM5 motherboards, DDR5 RAM, and even Ryzen 7000 CPUs have mostly come down in price significantly. The Ryzen 5 7600 might be selling for its $229 MSRP right now, but you'll most probably see a price cut when Black Friday is in full swing. This 6-core, 12-thread CPU is essentially a Ryzen 5 7600X with slightly lower clocks and a lower TDP.

In terms of gaming performance, this translates to an excellent 1440p experience nonetheless, with its 5.1GHz boost clock enough to go toe-to-toe with your graphics card (which I'll come to in a moment). Its 65W TDP will even let you get by with a barebones cooler if you don't want to buy a new one, although I highly recommend you do. Overall, if you want an upgradable platform that lets you drop in one or two CPU upgrades down the line, the Ryzen 5 7600 is one of the best gaming CPUs to base your build around.

Source: Thermalright
Thermalright Peerless Assassin 120 SE ARGB
Best CPU cooler

Incredible cooling on a budget

The Peerless Assassin 120 SE has been on our list of the best CPU coolers for good reason. It's highly capable, looks great, is more than enough for an overclocked Ryzen 5 7600. And the best part — it costs less than $40.

$35 at Amazon $35 at Newegg

While the Ryzen 5 7600 might not be a particularly power-hungry chip, there's still a lot of value to be had from a decent air cooler. And the Thermalright Peerless Assassin 120 SE is one of the best air coolers you can get for under $40. The dual-tower cooler comes with two 120mm fans capable of running at 1,500 RPM and generating 66 CFM of airflow. It's even rated for 265W, so you should have no complaints even with an overclocked Ryzen 5 7600.

The Peerless Assassin SE 120 looks impressive with its sleek black and chrome design and dual RGB fans, so you'll not miss a liquid AIO — either for performance or looks. Going with an air cooler for this build is also crucial to maximizing the value by spending elsewhere, especially when the CPU I've picked doesn't need a liquid cooler. You can still enjoy a silent and cool operation with this stellar air cooler.

Source: Zotac
ZOTAC Gaming GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Trinity OC
Best GPU

Enjoy the latest titles at Ultra settings

$785 $880 Save $95

The RTX 4070 Ti Trinity OC from Zotac is a monster graphics card in a $1,500 PC. You're getting a significant performance uplift compared to the RTX 4070 and a design that's simply sensational. It's capable of 1440p high-end and even decent 4K gaming.

$785 at Amazon $785 at Newegg

The graphics card I've picked for this unbeatable $1,500 gaming build is the Zotac Gaming GeForce RTX 4070 Ti Trinity OC. Not only is this one of the best RTX 4070 Ti graphics cards on the market, it offers incredible gaming performance for the price. Sitting between the RTX 4070 and RTX 4080, it enjoys a unique position — packing high-end GPU power without costing as much as an entire PC. Fun fact — the Zotac Trinity OC is also one of the only graphics cards for Cyberpunk 2077's path tracing mode. It's very likely to see a further price cut during Black Friday, so waiting is definitely worth it.

In addition to handling edge cases like Cyberpunk 2077 maxed out at 1440p (thanks to Frame Generation, DLSS 3.5, and Ray Reconstruction), the RTX 4070 Ti Trinity OC can even breach 100 FPS at 4K Ultra settings in many modern titles such as Watch Dogs: Legion, Forza Horizon 5, and Assassin's Creed Valhalla. You can pick an AMD GPU like the RX 7900 XT or even the older RX 6950 XT to reduce your overall budget, but I'd rather pick a solid ray tracing GPU when building a $1,500 budget.

Source: Newegg
ASRock B650M Pro RS WiFi
Best B650 motherboard

Great value and reliability

$125 $150 Save $25

The ASRock B650M Pro RS WiFi is one of the most well-rounded B650 motherboards you can buy. Despite its lower price and micro-ATX form factor, you're getting everything you need for a killer Ryzen 7000 build.

$125 at Newegg $125 at Walmart

For the motherboard, you don't need anything more than a decent B650 option. And while the ASRock B650M Pro RS WiFi is not one of the best AM5 motherboards, it certainly comes close. To maximize the value from this build and squeeze in that RTX 4070 Ti, I picked this micro-ATX option from ASRock that packs in all the necessary features you need. It even looks great and is bound to be in sync with the overall theme.

For a spectacular price of $125 (which is likely to remain through Black Friday), you're getting three M.2 slots, including a PCIe 5.0 slot. An 8-phase power design, Wi-Fi 6E, and Bluetooth 5.2 offer a competitive value proposition compared to many other boards. You're even getting a USB 3.2 Gen2 Type-C port and some RGB as well. The ASRock B650M Pro RS WiFi is known to provide a stable experience even in intense scenarios, making this one of the best deals you can get.

Source: Adata
ADATA XPG Lancer DDR5 RAM
Best RAM

Hits right in the Ryzen sweet spot

$105 $120 Save $15

The XPG Lancer RGB DDR5 6000 CL30 kit offers unbelievable performance and aesthetics for just around $100. You're getting 32GB of 6000MT/s CL30 RAM, which happens to be the sweet spot for the AM5 Ryzen processors.

$105 at Amazon $100 at Newegg

I believe that the graphics card, storage, PSU, and RAM are the components you shouldn't skimp on, especially on a $1,500 budget. In that vein, I've picked the Adata XPG Lancer RGB DDR5 32GB kit from for this build. The combo of 6000MT/s and CL30 is the sweet spot where you don't sacrifice performance too much and stop yourself from overspending as well. The kit comes AMD EXPO certified so you'll not face any stability issues either.

As for its looks, the kit offers a sleek black design with tasteful RGB. There's also a white option but the former looks just right for this build. Compared to many other kits from Corsair or G.Skill, this Adata kit provides arguably the best balance between price, performance, and design, making this one of the best DDR5 RAM on the market.

Samsung 980 Pro 2TB
Best SSD

Still one of the fastest SSDs

For any gaming build, 1TB might be just fine, but the wildly popular Samsung 980 Pro 2TB NVMe is currently retailing at nearly its all-time lowest price, and is an easy recommendation. It's an ultra-fast Gen4 SSD with 2GB DRAM and a 5-year warranty.

$120 at Amazon $130 at Newegg

When it comes to the best SSDs for gaming, the Samsung 980 Pro might have been superseded by its successor, the 990 Pro, but from a value-for-money standpoint, the 980 Pro is still a flagship-grade SSD. It comes with Samsung's best-in-class Gen4 performance, pleasing design, and hardware-based encryption. This is a DRAM SSD from one of the top players in the space and currently costs barely more than $100.

You might spot even better deals for the 980 Pro on Black Friday, making the overall package even sweeter. For a gaming SSD, it's hard to find a faster and more reliable option at this price. And even in your non-gaming workloads, its excellent sustained write performance will let you breeze through anything you throw at it.

MSI MAG A850GL
Best PSU

Excellent ATX 3.0 power for cheap

$110 $140 Save $30

MSI's MSG A850GL is an incredibly competitive ATX 3.0 and PCIe 5.0 PSU to power your future-ready Ryzen build. With a native 12VHPWR connector, rock-solid components, and 7-year warranty, this PSU is not kidding around.

$110 at Amazon $120 at Newegg

The latest ATX 3.0 power supplies started out being extremely expensive but have now settled down. To power this particular build, the MSI MAG A850GL is the perfect choice for more reasons than one. First, it's well-reviewed and extremely popular in the community, thanks to its quality construction, fully modular design, and future-ready features. Second, even though you don't absolutely need it in this build, the 850W of power sets you up comfortably for future GPU or CPU upgrades, And, third, it's priced extremely well.

For little more than $100 (which happens to be its lowest price ever on Amazon), you're ensuring clean and reliable power for your precious components, plus the native 12VHPWR connector translates to a hassle-free connection to your RTX 40-series graphics card. Come Black Friday, there might not be further reduction in this PSU's price, but you might just be able to snag the current deal even then.

Corsair 4000X
Best case

Compact yet powerful, great-looking PC case

$120 $145 Save $25

The Corsair 4000X RGB case might not be one of the cheapest out there, but its quality materials and design, plenty of airflow, built-in RGB fans, and easy cable management make it one of the best ones on the market.

$120 at Amazon $120 at Newegg

To end with, I picked the well-known Corsair 4000X RGB case to build this gaming PC in. It might not feature on our list of the best PC cases for gamers, but that doesn't mean it's not a great case. First off, you're getting a great-looking design with a side and front tempered glass to show off your build. Second, there's plenty of room and cable management features to make your build process smoother. Third, you're getting impressive airflow and hence, performance. And lastly, there are three RGB fans at the front already.

There are even more quality-of-life features in this excellent case. The side panels slide off easily, making the process tool-less. There's a screw mount for you to put in a vertical GPU mount if you prefer. And you can easily sync the RGB with Corsair's iCUE software. One of the only downsides of this case might be the lack of 360mm radiator support at the top (you can put a 280mm one). But the rest of the package is irresistible, especially at the current price.

Hold your breath till Black Friday

At the current prices, this build might not look like a $1,500 rig. But you can shave off around $100 once Black Friday deals are live. The CPU and GPU chosen will most likely see a price reduction, while the PSU and case are strong maybes. If you're not one for waiting, you can also check out the best gaming pre-builds for some fantastic options.

item.title: The Opal Tadpole is a super-sleek webcam made for laptops
item.itunes:title: The Opal Tadpole is a super-sleek webcam made for laptops
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • The Opal Tadpole is a compact and sleek webcam specifically designed for use with laptops, offering improved video quality for on-the-go calls.
  • With a 48MP Sony sensor and f/1.8 aperture, the Opal Tadpole delivers excellent image quality comparable to high-end smartphones.
  • The webcam's directional microphone, AI noise filtering, and touch-sensitive mute feature ensure clear audio during calls in noisy environments, making it ideal for use in public spaces.

The best webcams on the market tend to be pretty big and heavy, but Opal, a relative newcomer in the space, is shaking things up with the new Tadpole webcam. The company's latest webcam comes in an extremely sleek package, and it's made to be used specifically with laptops, so you can improve the quality of your calls on the go.

Because they're mostly designed for desktop setups, webcams can get pretty heavy and big (the Razer Kiyo Pro Ultra is a good example of that), but the Opal Tadpole measures a mere 1.2 inches squared, and it's very thin, too. Rather than using a traditional mount, it has a small clip, which is made to attach easily to the top of a laptop's screen rather than a thick monitor.

Image credit: Opal

Most of the body of the webcam is taken up by the camera lens, which is pretty big, since it features a 48MP Sony IMX582 sensor that can capture 4K video and has a six-element glass lens with f/1.8 aperture. Opal calls it a DSLR-quality camera, and it does look like it could be one of the best webcams on the market, seeing as they tend to have fairly weak cameras compared to, say, a high-end smartphone.

The Opal Tadpole also features what the company calls VisiMic technology. Essentially, the microphone on the webcam is completely directional, so it only hears the audio of what's in front of it. After all, if you're using the webcam with a laptop, you might be in a public space with a lot of noise, so the idea is that none of those distractions will come through the microphone during your calls. The camera also uses AI to help filter out noise and improve call quality. Since there's no room for a button with this design, Opal built a touch-sensitive surface into the USB plug of the webcam, and you can tap it to mute the microphone at any time.

Portability is obviously a big focus for the Opal Tadpole, and the USB cable itself can double as a wrist strap so you can easily carry it with you. Opal also designed a protective case if you want to keep the webcam safe while you're out and about, though it's sold separately.

The Opal Tadpole is available for purchase today, and it costs $175. That's definitely one of the more expensive cameras you can get, but we live in a world of $300 webcams, so if Opal's quality claims hold up, it might not be outrageously expensive.

Image credit: Opal
Opal Tadpole

The Opal Tadpole is a sleek webcam designed to be used with laptops. It features a 48MP Soony sensor with f/1.8 aperture and a six-element lens that should deliver excellent video quality.

$175 at Amazon

Since there's no room for a button with this design, Opal built a touch-sensitive surface into the USB plug of the webcam, and you can tap it to mute the microphone at any time.

Portability is obviously a big focus for the Opal Tadpole, and the USB cable itself can double as a wrist strap so you can easily carry it with you. Opal also designed a protective case if you want to keep the webcam safe while you're out and about, though it's sold separately.

The Opal Tadpole is available for purchase today, and it costs $175. That's definitely one of the more expensive cameras you can get, but we live in a world of $300 webcams, so if Opal's quality claims hold up, it might not be outrageously expensive.

Image credit: Opal
Opal Tadpole

The Opal Tadpole is a sleek webcam designed to be used with laptops. It features a 48MP Soony sensor with f/1.8 aperture and a six-element lens that should deliver excellent video quality.

$175 at Amazon

item.title: Ridiculous deal knocks up to $200 off M3 MacBook and iMac
item.itunes:title: Ridiculous deal knocks up to $200 off M3 MacBook and iMac
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The holiday shopping season is here, with big deals on some of the best laptops and desktops. Now, if you've been thinking about buying yourself a new Mac computer, right now's the perfect time with the release of the all-new M3 MacBook Pro and iMac. Both models have been refreshed with new processors, bringing added power in the form of the M3, M3 Pro, and M3 Max.

With that said, no matter which model you choose, you can't go wrong when it comes to power, it's really just a matter of if you need something portable, or just something for home. Right now, B&H is offering some big discounts on the MacBook Pro and iMac, knocking up to $200 off for a limited time.

  • MacBook Pro (M3, 2023)
    $1499 $1599 Save $100

    The new MacBook Pro offers Apple's M3, M3 Pro, and M3 Max chips in 14-inch and 16-inch designs. It features a 20% brighter display and an all-new Space Black finish option.

    $2299 at B&H (16 inches) $1499 at B&H (14 inches)
  • 24-inch iMac (M3, 2023)
    $1199 $1299 Save $100

    2023's 24-inch iMac receives a notable processor bump from Apple M1 to M3, along with Wi-Fi and Bluetooth upgrades. It retains the same exterior design and color options of its 2021 predecessor.

    $1199 at B&H

While the outer chassis of the MacBook Pro remains the same as the previous model, the internals get a boost thanks to the new M3 chip which offers more power at up to 80% when compared to the M1 Max, which's perfect for rendering projects, playing games, and more. In addition to the added processing performance, the new M3 chip is also more efficient, providing up to 22 hours of battery life on a single charge. Furthermore, the new MacBook Pro gets a new color this year with the release of the Space Gray variant.

When it comes to the iMac, you're getting a beautiful 24-inch 4.5K Retina display and computer that's now offered in seven vibrant and playful colors. The computer is powered by Apple's M3 chip, giving it lots of power, so it can handle pretty much anything you can throw at it. For the most part, you're getting a really good computer no matter which one you choose, and since they are now discounted, it's even better value. Just be sure to pick one up while you can because this deal won't last long.

item.title: How to make your GPU perform better
item.itunes:title: How to make your GPU perform better
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Graphics cards are one of the most expensive components in your computer, so it can be a daunting process to have to buy a new one. Luckily, it is possible to improve your current graphics card to make it last a little longer and perform better in newer games. There are several ways that you can improve the performance of your graphics card, but before you start opening components, changing settings within your computer's BIOS, or overclocking anything, try these simple methods first.

Is your computer bottlenecking?

Source: Unsplash

Bottlenecking is the first thing to consider when you want to improve your GPU's performance. A bottleneck occurs when one of the components on your computer is slower than the rest. For instance, your graphics card may be underperforming because your processor is slow, or you might not have enough RAM for your graphics card to run smoothly. A bottleneck can be hard to pinpoint, but before you consider buying any components for your computer, you should list your current components and look for any hardware that may be holding the system back.

Cleaning and maintenance

Over time, your computer will accumulate dust, and if you don't clean the fans and vents regularly, this will block airflow to the computer, causing it to overheat and perform worse. It is recommended to clean your computer every three to six months depending on how often you use it, but even cleaning your computer once a year can help boost your GPU's performance and increase the longevity of your card and other components. On the reverse, if you find yourself cleaning your computer very frequently or you notice your PC is overheating often, then your case does not have enough ventilation, so you’ll need more fans to direct the airflow and more air vents.

In-game settings and resolution

Sometimes it's not your graphics card, sometimes it's the game. Every game you play will be different and will have different graphical requirements, but you can change your resolution and in-game settings to allow the game to play smoothly with little change in the overall quality.

Lowering your in-game graphics settings or tweaking very specific settings within the game can be one of the easiest methods to make your graphics card last longer. In many games, the visual differences between low and ultra settings are night and day, but the difference between high and ultra settings may be harder to spot and can unnecessarily push your hardware for very little graphical effect.

Another setting that may cause your GPU to slow down is the resolution you are using in the game. A game that runs on high/ultra settings at 1080p may need to be lowered to medium/low settings when played in 4K. A general rule of thumb: Every time you double your resolution, you should halve your graphics settings.

Nvidia or AMD graphics cards?

Whether you have an AMD or Nvidia graphics card will affect what applications you use to improve your GPU. Computers with AMD graphics cards have a built-in program called Radeon Settings that allows you to monitor your card's performance and tweak settings within the graphics card safely.

Nvidia's program, GeForce Experience, allows users to optimize in-game settings on their games and also has the option to record gameplay. Nvidia's graphics cards also have access to the Nvidia control panel, which hosts a myriad of resolutions and advanced display settings for you to customize.

Nvidia and AMD use these applications to perform maintenance on cards and provide users with software updates and drivers that improve their graphics card's performance in newer games. Generally, driver updates are automated by the computer, but if your computer doesn't have access to the internet to update, then many of the games you play will become slower.

Updating your Nvidia graphics card drivers

  1. Search for or open the Nvidia GeForce Experience application.
  2. Once the program opens, select the Drivers tab.
  3. Click on Check for updates and download the latest driver.
  4. Look at the version and release date to confirm that it is the latest update.

Updating your AMD graphics card drivers

  1. Search for and open your Device Manager.
  2. Select your display adapter, then right-click on the AMD graphics driver and click Update driver.
  3. Select the search automatically for drivers option.
  4. The driver will automatically download, or a prompt will appear saying you have the latest driver.

Overclocking your graphics card

Overclocking is the process of changing the settings on your graphics card to make it run faster and perform better. This may sound like a cheat code to improve your graphics within a game, but be warned that the process can damage your graphics card or motherboard and reduce the lifespan of your components. Regardless of the risk, many gamers overclock their graphics cards to squeeze a few more frames out of older cards.

The process of overclocking a graphics card is different for every card and different companies have different stances on the matter. Nvidia doesn't particularly like overclocking — you can still do it — but it is intentionally harder to overclock than AMD cards, which often have overclocking settings built into the graphics card. Learning how to manually overclock your GPU is difficult, and if you are inexperienced, then you could overheat and damage your card.

The safest tool to use when overclocking your graphics card is MSI Afterburner, an industry-standard software that works on both Nvidia and AMD. The software identifies your components and allows you to overclock your components without damaging them.

How to overclock your Graphics card with MSI Afterburner

  1. Search for MSI Afterburner.
  2. Click Download Afterburner and open the installation folder.
  3. Click Next to Install MSI Afterburner.
  4. Tick the box for the Rivatuner statistics server.
  5. Click Next to Install the Rivatuner statistics server.
  6. Once MSI Afterburner is open, you will be able to change the voltage, clock, and fan speeds to improve the performance of your graphics card.

Final thoughts

How old your graphics card and components are is the biggest factor to consider when looking to improve your GPU's performance. A computer that is five years old at this point should be able to play most modern games on medium settings and would probably only benefit from a storage and RAM upgrade. But if your graphics card is older than that, and you can only play games at minimum settings, then it might be worthwhile to try to overclock your graphics card until you can upgrade your PC with a better graphics card at a later point.

item.title: Don't miss out on huge savings on our favorite Fire TV devices, Echo Show speakers, smart TVs and more
item.itunes:title: Don't miss out on huge savings on our favorite Fire TV devices, Echo Show speakers, smart TVs and more
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
We're just a couple of weeks away from Black Friday but that doesn't mean we have to wait that long to find great deals. Amazon's been on a roll lately with its sales, starting back in October with its Prime Big Deal Days sales event, and since rolling out new deals pretty much every day for the month of November.

With that said, the online retailer is now pulling out all of the stops, slashing prices on all its popular products, with deep discounts on Fire TV streaming sticks, Echo Shows, Fire smart TVs, Kindle, and more. So if you've been thinking about buying something for yourself, or just wanted to grab something as sa gift, now's going to be the perfect time to buy.

Fire TV

Fire TVs are an excellent choice if you're looking for great value. Amazon offers a wide selection of TVs, and Fire TVs are also manufactured by others brands as well. While the technology in TVs can be different, the underlying OS still provides a wonderful experience with easy access to popular streaming services like Netflix, Prime Video, Hulu, HBO's Max, and more.

  • Amazon Fire TV 55" 4-Series 4K UHD smart TV
    $340 $520 Save $180

    The Amazon Fire TV 4-Series provides vibrant colors and excellent smart features, providing easy access to your favorite streaming video services. Right now, this TV is on sale, dropping well below its retail price for a limited time.

    $340 at Amazon
  • Amazon Fire TV 50" 4-Series 4K UHD smart TV
    $290 $450 Save $160

    The Amazon Fire TV 4-Series provides vibrant colors and excellent smart features, providing easy access to your favorite streaming video services. Right now, this TV is on sale, dropping well below its retail price for a limited time.

    $290 at Amazon
  • Amazon Fire TV 43-inch 4-Series 4K UHD Smart TV
    $250 $370 Save $120

    The Amazon Fire TV 4-Series provides vibrant colors and excellent smart features, providing easy access to your favorite streaming video services. Right now, this TV is on sale, dropping well below its retail price for a limited time.

    $250 at Amazon
  • Amazon Fire TV 55" Omni QLED Series 4K UHD smart TV (Save 27%)
  • Amazon Fire TV 65" Omni QLED Series 4K UHD smart TV (Save 26%)
  • Amazon Fire TV 75" Omni QLED Series 4K UHD smart TV (Save 20%)
Insignia F20 Series Smart Full HD 1080p Fire TV
32-inch model
$120 $170 Save $50

A small 1080p that also packs quite a punch with its enhanced feature set. Right now you can score this TV for an absolute steal at just $120.

$120 at Amazon

Fire Stick TV

If you need a fast and easy way to breathe life into an existing TV, a streaming media stick is the perfect solution. While there are lots of options to choose from, Amazon's Fire TV Sticks are some of the best, providing a wide selection of devices at a range of different prices. While there are notable differences between models, regardless of which one you choose, you're going to get a great experience. And right now, you're going to be able to save big on Fire TV Sticks thanks to the stellar deals available.

  • Amazon Fire TV Stick Lite
    $18 $30 Save $12

    The Fire TV Stick Lite is a budget-friendly variant of the Fire TV that provides most of the features available in its older sibling.

    $18 at Amazon
  • Amazon Fire TV Stick 4K

    The Fire TV Stick 4K provides a beautiful 4K resolution, and offers easy access to popular streaming services and games.

    $50 at Amazon
  • Amazon Fire TV Stick 4K Max

    The Fire TV 4K Max is one of the best streaming media sticks available, offering 4K video, support for Wi-Fi 6E, and easy access to popular streaming services and games.

    $60 at Amazon

Echo speakers

Whether it's your first or third smart speaker, you really can't go wrong with Amazon's Echo Show lineup. The brand offers a wide range of options at nearly every price point. Furthermore, you can now get them at a steal thanks to new discounts that drop prices down to their lowest yet.

Amazon Echo Show 8 (2nd Gen)
$70 $130 Save $60

The Echo Show 8 (2nd Gen) is the latest smart speaker from Amazon in India and comes with an 8-inch display and a 13MP camera for video calls.

$70 at Amazon

Fire tablets

Source: Amazon
Amazon Fire HD 10 (2021)

This tablet is great for light usage, such as browsing the web, watching movies, reading news, attending remote classes, and more.

    $250 $370 Save $120

    The Amazon Fire TV 4-Series provides vibrant colors and excellent smart features, providing easy access to your favorite streaming video services. Right now, this TV is on sale, dropping well below its retail price for a limited time.

    $250 at Amazon
  • Amazon Fire TV 55" Omni QLED Series 4K UHD smart TV (Save 27%)
  • Amazon Fire TV 65" Omni QLED Series 4K UHD smart TV (Save 26%)
  • Amazon Fire TV 75" Omni QLED Series 4K UHD smart TV (Save 20%)
Insignia F20 Series Smart Full HD 1080p Fire TV
32-inch model
$120 $170 Save $50

A small 1080p that also packs quite a punch with its enhanced feature set. Right now you can score this TV for an absolute steal at just $120.

$120 at Amazon

Fire Stick TV

If you need a fast and easy way to breathe life into an existing TV, a streaming media stick is the perfect solution. While there are lots of options to choose from, Amazon's Fire TV Sticks are some of the best, providing a wide selection of devices at a range of different prices. While there are notable differences between models, regardless of which one you choose, you're going to get a great experience. And right now, you're going to be able to save big on Fire TV Sticks thanks to the stellar deals available.

  • Amazon Fire TV Stick Lite
    $18 $30 Save $12

    The Fire TV Stick Lite is a budget-friendly variant of the Fire TV that provides most of the features available in its older sibling.

    $18 at Amazon
  • Amazon Fire TV Stick 4K

    The Fire TV Stick 4K provides a beautiful 4K resolution, and offers easy access to popular streaming services and games.

    $50 at Amazon
  • Amazon Fire TV Stick 4K Max

    The Fire TV 4K Max is one of the best streaming media sticks available, offering 4K video, support for Wi-Fi 6E, and easy access to popular streaming services and games.

    $60 at Amazon

Echo speakers

Whether it's your first or third smart speaker, you really can't go wrong with Amazon's Echo Show lineup. The brand offers a wide range of options at nearly every price point. Furthermore, you can now get them at a steal thanks to new discounts that drop prices down to their lowest yet.

Amazon Echo Show 8 (2nd Gen)
$70 $130 Save $60

The Echo Show 8 (2nd Gen) is the latest smart speaker from Amazon in India and comes with an 8-inch display and a 13MP camera for video calls.

$70 at Amazon

Fire tablets

Source: Amazon
Amazon Fire HD 10 (2021)

This tablet is great for light usage, such as browsing the web, watching movies, reading news, attending remote classes, and more.
item.content:encoded:
We're just a couple of weeks away from Black Friday but that doesn't mean we have to wait that long to find great deals. Amazon's been on a roll lately with its sales, starting back in October with its Prime Big Deal Days sales event, and since rolling out new deals pretty much every day for the month of November.

With that said, the online retailer is now pulling out all of the stops, slashing prices on all its popular products, with deep discounts on Fire TV streaming sticks, Echo Shows, Fire smart TVs, Kindle, and more. So if you've been thinking about buying something for yourself, or just wanted to grab something as sa gift, now's going to be the perfect time to buy.

Fire TV

Fire TVs are an excellent choice if you're looking for great value. Amazon offers a wide selection of TVs, and Fire TVs are also manufactured by others brands as well. While the technology in TVs can be different, the underlying OS still provides a wonderful experience with easy access to popular streaming services like Netflix, Prime Video, Hulu, HBO's Max, and more.

item.title: Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB review: Fast and pricey
item.itunes:title: Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB review: Fast and pricey
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The field of second-generation PCIe 5.0 SSDs is gradually becoming more crowded, bringing more options for users who want drives capable of 12,000MB/s reads and writes. Corsair's MP700 Pro 2TB, the successor to the MP700 that launched earlier this year, is the latest contender to vie for the title of best PCIe 5.0 SSD, and offers three models: one with no heatsink, one with an air-cooled heatsink, and one with a liquid-cooled heatsink. On paper, it's the most versatile and perhaps most performant PCIe 5.0 SSD thus far.

The MP700 Pro 2TB is the fastest PCIe 5.0 SSD I've ever tested, instantly making it a must-have for performance chasers. However, its relatively high price prevents it from being very competitive with other second-generation PCIe 5.0 drives. Additionally, its two optional heatsinks aren't big selling points, as the air-cooled model suffers from a very poor design choice while liquid cooling for SSDs is hard to recommend since it's so niche. While an obvious choice for the high rolling enthusiast, the MP700 Pro 2TB's premium price is hard to justify when cheaper SSDs with similar performance exist.

About this review: Corsair sent me the MP700 Pro 2TB for the purposes of this review. Corsair did not see the contents of this review before publishing.

Source: Corsair
Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB
Great but a bit too expensive

Top-end performance with a premium that's not quite worth it

8 / 10

Corsair's MP700 Pro 2TB is the company's flagship second-generation PCIe 5.0 SSD. It's rated for 12GB/s reads and writes, and you can pick it up with an air cooler or a waterblock for liquid coolers.

Storage capacity
2TB
Hardware Interface
PCIe 5.0
Transfer rate
12,400/11,800MB/s reads/writes
Pros
  • Fastest PCIe 5.0 SSD so far
  • Great sustained writing performance
  • Optional air cooler or waterblock for liquid coolers
Cons
  • Price tag is difficult to justify
  • Air cooler always runs at 100% fan speed and is annoying
$325 at Amazon $325 at Newegg

Corsair MP700 Pro: Pricing and availability

The MP700 Pro launched on Nov. 14, and it comes in 1TB and 2TB sizes. Both the 1TB and 2TB models of the MP700 Pro can be bought with a preinstalled air cooler, and there's also a variant of the 2TB drive with a waterblock installed for custom liquid cooling loops. Without coolers, the MP700 Pro 1TB costs $180, while the 2TB costs $300. The cooled models (both air and liquid) bump the price up to $190 for the 1TB and $325 for the 2TB.

For enthusiasts with high-end PCs, the MP700 Pro 2TB is especially appealing as it's the only PCIe 5.0 SSD that can optionally come with a preinstalled waterblock.

While PCIe 5.0 SSDs are expected to be pretty expensive, the MP700 Pro is especially expensive among its peers. Crucial's T700 retails at $290 and $160 for the 2TB and 1TB variants respectively, while Teamgroup's T-Force Cardea Z540 is even cheaper at $260 for 2TB and $150 for 1TB. Granted, the T700 only offers a passive heatsink while the Z540 comes with none at all, but separately sold coolers are available at Amazon for $10 to $20, which diminishes the advantage Corsair has with its cooler options.

Performance

How the Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB was tested

Corsair sent me the MP700 Pro 2TB with the air cooler preinstalled, and for this review I tested it as-is. I also have performance figures for the Z540 2TB, Gigabyte's Gen5 12000 1TB, and Seagate's FireCuda 540 2TB, which is a first-generation 10,000MB/s drive. These drives were tested on my Intel test bench, which uses a Core i9-14900K, ASRock Z790 Taichi Lite, and 32GB of DDR5 running at 5,600MHz and CL40 timings. On AMD PCs, PCIe 5.0 SSD performance is usually a little lower than it is on Intel PCs, though not by a massive margin.

I tested all SSDs in three benchmarks to assess overall performance: CrystalDiskMark, 3DMark's storage test, and IOMeter. These programs test peak performance, gaming performance, and sustained writing performance respectively, and they're probably the three most important things to look for in an SSD beyond price and capacity. To prevent results from being impacted by depleted SSD cache or thermal throttling, all SSDs had a rest period of 10 minutes or more between each test.

The Z540 2TB, Gen5 12000 1TB, and FireCuda 540 2TB were tested under the Z790 Taichi Lite's stock heatsink, as none of them came preinstalled with a heatsink. However, since the Gen5 12000 does come with a heatsink that you can optionally install yourself, I have noted down where it improves performance and by how much, which is just in a single test as it turns out. Otherwise, cooling didn't impact the performance results.

CrystalDiskMark

CrystalDiskMark is a synthetic benchmarking app that tests data transfer rates under a variety of conditions. I've tested the six default tests, which measure performance in sequential and random workloads across different block sizes, queue depths, and thread counts. While this performance isn't exactly real world, it can still tell us the peak capabilities of a drive's performance.

MP700 Pro 2TB

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

SEQ1M Q8T1

12,389/11,666

12,391/11,701

11,682/9,537

10,073/10,197

SEQ1M Q1T1

9,255/9,610

9,297/9,636

9,169/9,216

8,601/9,622

SEQ128K Q32T1

12,296/11,467

12,281/11,479

11,471/9,563

9,778/10,151

RND4K Q32T16

6,390/6,839

6,382/7,031

5,697/6,536

6,106/6,797

RND4K Q32T1

1,114/828

1,169/858

1,178/851

1,155/839

RND4K Q1T1

100/392

101/393

101/397

100/385

Scores are organized by read/write and are measured in MB/s.

As expected, the MP700 Pro 2TB and Z540 2TB are neck and neck as they use the same NAND chips and controller. Meanwhile, the Gen5 12000 1TB is a fair bit behind in reads and especially writes in three of the workloads, which is typical for the 1TB versions of 12,000MB/s SSDs. All SSDs however are neck and neck in the lower thread count random tests, which don't particularly benefit from higher performance hardware.

Although the Z540 2TB was tested under the Z790 Taichi Lite's stock heatsink, it performed the same as the actively cooled MP700 Pro 2TB. This is because CrystalDiskMark just doesn't run long enough to saturate the heatsink with heat, so the Z540 2TB can maintain peak speed right alongside the MP700 Pro. For longer benchmarks, you'll see that this isn't the case.

3DMark

3DMark's storage benchmark runs real world workloads from popular (albeit aging) games like Overwatch, and assigns a score based on overall performance in data transfer and latency.

MP700 Pro 2TB

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

Score

5,593

5,783

5,772

5,620

The MP700 Pro 2TB is in 4th place, but it's by such a small margin that all SSDs here are effectively tied. Additionally, 3DMark's current storage benchmark is likely not suitable for testing PCIe 5.0 SSDs, as it predates DirectStorage and primarily tests loading times. I suspect 14,000MB/s SSDs won't do much better than 12,000MB/s drives like the MP700 Pro, as the MP700 Pro doesn't do much better than the FireCuda 540 2TB and other 10,000MB/s drives.

IOMeter

IOMeter is the final benchmark I have to show, and I use it to test how well an SSD can write over 15 minutes. When an SSD spends a long time writing data, two things happen. Firstly, its high-performance cache is eventually depleted, which can happen in less than a minute of writing. Secondly, the more an SSD is filled up, the weaker writing performance becomes, as there are fewer empty blocks to dump data into, forcing the SSD to spend resources reorganizing data. I've set up IOMeter to show the performance impact of these two important factors.

First, here's the long-term writing performance of the four SSDs when they're filled up 50%.

MP700 Pro 2TB

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

Average Write Speed

3,814

3,613

2,082

3,816

Scores are measured in MB/s.

The MP700 Pro 2TB is neck-and-neck with the FireCuda 540 2TB on average, while the Z540 2TB follows closely behind. The performance benefits of newer PCIe 5.0 NAND and controllers is diminished over the course of a 15-minute test and with the drives filled halfway, which is how the FireCuda 540 2TB can keep up so well. Meanwhile, the Gen5 12000 1TB is in dead last as it has fewer chips and consequently worse bandwidth.

This next graph shows how the performance of the MP700 Pro 2TB changes depending on how full it is. I tested it at 10% full, 50% full (the same data from the last test), and 90% full, and I also included the average speeds for the MP700 Pro 2TB alongside the other SSDs.

MP700 Pro 2TB

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

10% full

11,518

9,329

8,262

9,000

50% full

3,814

3,609

2,082

3,812

90% full

3,680

2,721

1,912

3,583

Scores are measured in MB/s.

At 10% full, the MP700 Pro 2TB is able to sustain a consistent write speed of nearly 12,000MB/s, well ahead of the other SSDs. However, that's down to cooling, which is a big factor in long, sustained writing workloads. If you use a cooler with the Z540 2TB, you can expect it to be just about as fast as the MP700 Pro 2TB, while the Gen5 12000 1TB gets a boost to roughly 9,400MB/s.

At the much higher fill rate of 90%, the Z540 2TB loses a significant amount of ground that puts the MP700 Pro 2TB firmly in first place, ahead of the FireCuda 540 2TB too. Although the Z540 2TB uses very similar hardware, it loses a significant amount of ground from the 50% full test to the 90% full test. It seems that the MP700 Pro 2TB has much better cache replenishment, as thermal throttling didn't seem to be a factor with the Z540 2TB.

This drive has great sustained writing performance and the typical 700TBW per terabyte endurance, so it's a great choice for anyone who finds themselves doing tons of writing operations.

Cooler performance and thermals

Lastly, I want to discuss thermals, which are becoming increasingly important as PCIe 5.0 SSDs continue to run at high temperatures at peak performance. Without good cooling, an SSD has to thermal throttle, which means reduced reads and writes. The MP700 Pro's optional air cooler is interesting, as it doesn't use heatpipes or metal fins like the Gen5 12000's passive heatsink or Teamgroup's T-Force Dark Airflow I air-cooled heatsink. Instead, the MP700 Pro combines a more typical SSD heatsink with a fan that channels air along its length.

I ran IOMeter with the drive filled up 10% to make the MP700 Pro 2TB heat up as much as possible, and its temperature leveled out at a cool 64 C. This allowed it to never thermal throttle and maintain peak writing performance of 11,500MB/s during the entire 15-minute-long test. The Z540 2TB on the other hand did thermal throttle under the Z790 Taichi Lite's stock heatsink. Under the T-Force Dark Airflow I however, the Z540 2TB was able to maintain over 11,000MB/s just like the MP700 Pro 2TB.

There was just one problem with the MP700 Pro's cooler: the cable. For some reason, Corsair decided to hook this fan up to a SATA power connector, which is a design choice I don't understand. SATA-powered fans always run at 100% and can't be controlled by software or firmware, and although the MP700 Pro's fan isn't very loud, it is high-pitched. Even a 3-pin fan plug would have been better since at least with those, you can attach a resistor cable to reduce the fan speed. If the fan just used a normal 4-pin plug, I would have called it the best PCIe 5.0 SSD cooler so far.

Should you buy the Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB?

You should buy the Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB if:

  • You want the fastest PCIe 5.0 SSD right now
  • You like the idea of liquid cooling an SSD
  • You have a large budget

You shouldn't buy the Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB if:

  • You want a PCIe 5.0 SSD with good bang for buck
  • You want a PCIe 5.0 SSD with a good overall cooling solution
  • You don't want to wait for next-generation PCIe 5.0 SSDs

Overall, the MP700 Pro 2TB is a great SSD. When adequately cooled, it has the best performance you can get out of a PCIe 5.0 SSD, and it offers three models that should cover the needs and wants of any user. For enthusiasts with high-end PCs, the MP700 Pro 2TB is especially appealing as it's the only PCIe 5.0 SSD that can optionally come with a preinstalled waterblock.

Unfortunately, the MP700 Pro just doesn't do enough to really stand out and justify its price tag for the average PCIe 5.0 drive buyer. It performs pretty much the same as the Z540 while costing significantly more, and although an extra $40 isn't going to break the bank for someone in the market for a PCIe 5.0 SSD, it's still $40 that could be used for something else. If you're buying multiple PCIe 5.0 drives, $40 for each one will add up quickly.

The MP700 Pro 2TB is the fastest PCIe 5.0 SSD I've ever tested.

The cooler options also aren't as killer in practice as they are on paper, since the air-cooled heatsink uses a disappointing fan powered by SATA, and liquid cooling an SSD is both overkill and applicable to only a very small slice of the PC community, even among high-end users. If Corsair is going to charge a $65 premium for the air cooler, it can at least use a fan with a standard 4-pin connector. Nobody building a PC that costs over $1,000 wants to listen to a whiny fan, and for me it ruins what would have been a nearly perfect SSD cooler.

Still, I think the MP700 Pro is worth getting if you want the best performance, you just need to get a third-party cooler for it or use the liquid cooled model. You'll still be paying significantly more for the MP700 Pro than other SSDs, but you can at least enjoy the best sustained writing performance today, and if you find yourself doing lots of writing workloads, then a premium might be worth it.

Source: Corsair
Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB
Great but a bit too expensive

Corsair's MP700 Pro 2TB is the company's flagship second-generation PCIe 5.0 SSD. It's rated for 12GB/s reads and writes, and you can pick it up with an air cooler or a waterblock for liquid coolers.

The MP700 Pro 2TB is neck-and-neck with the FireCuda 540 2TB on average, while the Z540 2TB follows closely behind. The performance benefits of newer PCIe 5.0 NAND and controllers is diminished over the course of a 15-minute test and with the drives filled halfway, which is how the FireCuda 540 2TB can keep up so well. Meanwhile, the Gen5 12000 1TB is in dead last as it has fewer chips and consequently worse bandwidth.

This next graph shows how the performance of the MP700 Pro 2TB changes depending on how full it is. I tested it at 10% full, 50% full (the same data from the last test), and 90% full, and I also included the average speeds for the MP700 Pro 2TB alongside the other SSDs.

MP700 Pro 2TB

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

10% full

11,518

9,329

8,262

9,000

50% full

3,814

3,609

2,082

3,812

90% full

3,680

2,721

1,912

3,583

Scores are measured in MB/s.

At 10% full, the MP700 Pro 2TB is able to sustain a consistent write speed of nearly 12,000MB/s, well ahead of the other SSDs. However, that's down to cooling, which is a big factor in long, sustained writing workloads. If you use a cooler with the Z540 2TB, you can expect it to be just about as fast as the MP700 Pro 2TB, while the Gen5 12000 1TB gets a boost to roughly 9,400MB/s.

At the much higher fill rate of 90%, the Z540 2TB loses a significant amount of ground that puts the MP700 Pro 2TB firmly in first place, ahead of the FireCuda 540 2TB too. Although the Z540 2TB uses very similar hardware, it loses a significant amount of ground from the 50% full test to the 90% full test. It seems that the MP700 Pro 2TB has much better cache replenishment, as thermal throttling didn't seem to be a factor with the Z540 2TB.

This drive has great sustained writing performance and the typical 700TBW per terabyte endurance, so it's a great choice for anyone who finds themselves doing tons of writing operations.

Cooler performance and thermals

Lastly, I want to discuss thermals, which are becoming increasingly important as PCIe 5.0 SSDs continue to run at high temperatures at peak performance. Without good cooling, an SSD has to thermal throttle, which means reduced reads and writes. The MP700 Pro's optional air cooler is interesting, as it doesn't use heatpipes or metal fins like the Gen5 12000's passive heatsink or Teamgroup's T-Force Dark Airflow I air-cooled heatsink. Instead, the MP700 Pro combines a more typical SSD heatsink with a fan that channels air along its length.

I ran IOMeter with the drive filled up 10% to make the MP700 Pro 2TB heat up as much as possible, and its temperature leveled out at a cool 64 C. This allowed it to never thermal throttle and maintain peak writing performance of 11,500MB/s during the entire 15-minute-long test. The Z540 2TB on the other hand did thermal throttle under the Z790 Taichi Lite's stock heatsink. Under the T-Force Dark Airflow I however, the Z540 2TB was able to maintain over 11,000MB/s just like the MP700 Pro 2TB.

There was just one problem with the MP700 Pro's cooler: the cable. For some reason, Corsair decided to hook this fan up to a SATA power connector, which is a design choice I don't understand. SATA-powered fans always run at 100% and can't be controlled by software or firmware, and although the MP700 Pro's fan isn't very loud, it is high-pitched. Even a 3-pin fan plug would have been better since at least with those, you can attach a resistor cable to reduce the fan speed. If the fan just used a normal 4-pin plug, I would have called it the best PCIe 5.0 SSD cooler so far.

Should you buy the Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB?

You should buy the Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB if:

  • You want the fastest PCIe 5.0 SSD right now
  • You like the idea of liquid cooling an SSD
  • You have a large budget

You shouldn't buy the Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB if:

  • You want a PCIe 5.0 SSD with good bang for buck
  • You want a PCIe 5.0 SSD with a good overall cooling solution
  • You don't want to wait for next-generation PCIe 5.0 SSDs

Overall, the MP700 Pro 2TB is a great SSD. When adequately cooled, it has the best performance you can get out of a PCIe 5.0 SSD, and it offers three models that should cover the needs and wants of any user. For enthusiasts with high-end PCs, the MP700 Pro 2TB is especially appealing as it's the only PCIe 5.0 SSD that can optionally come with a preinstalled waterblock.

Unfortunately, the MP700 Pro just doesn't do enough to really stand out and justify its price tag for the average PCIe 5.0 drive buyer. It performs pretty much the same as the Z540 while costing significantly more, and although an extra $40 isn't going to break the bank for someone in the market for a PCIe 5.0 SSD, it's still $40 that could be used for something else. If you're buying multiple PCIe 5.0 drives, $40 for each one will add up quickly.

The MP700 Pro 2TB is the fastest PCIe 5.0 SSD I've ever tested.

The cooler options also aren't as killer in practice as they are on paper, since the air-cooled heatsink uses a disappointing fan powered by SATA, and liquid cooling an SSD is both overkill and applicable to only a very small slice of the PC community, even among high-end users. If Corsair is going to charge a $65 premium for the air cooler, it can at least use a fan with a standard 4-pin connector. Nobody building a PC that costs over $1,000 wants to listen to a whiny fan, and for me it ruins what would have been a nearly perfect SSD cooler.

Still, I think the MP700 Pro is worth getting if you want the best performance, you just need to get a third-party cooler for it or use the liquid cooled model. You'll still be paying significantly more for the MP700 Pro than other SSDs, but you can at least enjoy the best sustained writing performance today, and if you find yourself doing lots of writing workloads, then a premium might be worth it.

Source: Corsair
Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB
Great but a bit too expensive

Corsair's MP700 Pro 2TB is the company's flagship second-generation PCIe 5.0 SSD. It's rated for 12GB/s reads and writes, and you can pick it up with an air cooler or a waterblock for liquid coolers.
item.content:encoded:
The field of second-generation PCIe 5.0 SSDs is gradually becoming more crowded, bringing more options for users who want drives capable of 12,000MB/s reads and writes. Corsair's MP700 Pro 2TB, the successor to the MP700 that launched earlier this year, is the latest contender to vie for the title of best PCIe 5.0 SSD, and offers three models: one with no heatsink, one with an air-cooled heatsink, and one with a liquid-cooled heatsink. On paper, it's the most versatile and perhaps most performant PCIe 5.0 SSD thus far.

The MP700 Pro 2TB is the fastest PCIe 5.0 SSD I've ever tested, instantly making it a must-have for performance chasers. However, its relatively high price prevents it from being very competitive with other second-generation PCIe 5.0 drives. Additionally, its two optional heatsinks aren't big selling points, as the air-cooled model suffers from a very poor design choice while liquid cooling for SSDs is hard to recommend since it's so niche. While an obvious choice for the high rolling enthusiast, the MP700 Pro 2TB's premium price is hard to justify when cheaper SSDs with similar performance exist.

About this review: Corsair sent me the MP700 Pro 2TB for the purposes of this review. Corsair did not see the contents of this review before publishing.

Source: Corsair
Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB
Great but a bit too expensive

Top-end performance with a premium that's not quite worth it

8 / 10

Corsair's MP700 Pro 2TB is the company's flagship second-generation PCIe 5.0 SSD. It's rated for 12GB/s reads and writes, and you can pick it up with an air cooler or a waterblock for liquid coolers.

Storage capacity
2TB
Hardware Interface
PCIe 5.0
Transfer rate
12,400/11,800MB/s reads/writes
Pros
  • Fastest PCIe 5.0 SSD so far
  • Great sustained writing performance
  • Optional air cooler or waterblock for liquid coolers
Cons
  • Price tag is difficult to justify
  • Air cooler always runs at 100% fan speed and is annoying
$325 at Amazon $325 at Newegg

Corsair MP700 Pro: Pricing and availability

The MP700 Pro launched on Nov. 14, and it comes in 1TB and 2TB sizes. Both the 1TB and 2TB models of the MP700 Pro can be bought with a preinstalled air cooler, and there's also a variant of the 2TB drive with a waterblock installed for custom liquid cooling loops. Without coolers, the MP700 Pro 1TB costs $180, while the 2TB costs $300. The cooled models (both air and liquid) bump the price up to $190 for the 1TB and $325 for the 2TB.

For enthusiasts with high-end PCs, the MP700 Pro 2TB is especially appealing as it's the only PCIe 5.0 SSD that can optionally come with a preinstalled waterblock.

While PCIe 5.0 SSDs are expected to be pretty expensive, the MP700 Pro is especially expensive among its peers. Crucial's T700 retails at $290 and $160 for the 2TB and 1TB variants respectively, while Teamgroup's T-Force Cardea Z540 is even cheaper at $260 for 2TB and $150 for 1TB. Granted, the T700 only offers a passive heatsink while the Z540 comes with none at all, but separately sold coolers are available at Amazon for $10 to $20, which diminishes the advantage Corsair has with its cooler options.

Performance

How the Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB was tested

Corsair sent me the MP700 Pro 2TB with the air cooler preinstalled, and for this review I tested it as-is. I also have performance figures for the Z540 2TB, Gigabyte's Gen5 12000 1TB, and Seagate's FireCuda 540 2TB, which is a first-generation 10,000MB/s drive. These drives were tested on my Intel test bench, which uses a Core i9-14900K, ASRock Z790 Taichi Lite, and 32GB of DDR5 running at 5,600MHz and CL40 timings. On AMD PCs, PCIe 5.0 SSD performance is usually a little lower than it is on Intel PCs, though not by a massive margin.

I tested all SSDs in three benchmarks to assess overall performance: CrystalDiskMark, 3DMark's storage test, and IOMeter. These programs test peak performance, gaming performance, and sustained writing performance respectively, and they're probably the three most important things to look for in an SSD beyond price and capacity. To prevent results from being impacted by depleted SSD cache or thermal throttling, all SSDs had a rest period of 10 minutes or more between each test.

The Z540 2TB, Gen5 12000 1TB, and FireCuda 540 2TB were tested under the Z790 Taichi Lite's stock heatsink, as none of them came preinstalled with a heatsink. However, since the Gen5 12000 does come with a heatsink that you can optionally install yourself, I have noted down where it improves performance and by how much, which is just in a single test as it turns out. Otherwise, cooling didn't impact the performance results.

CrystalDiskMark

CrystalDiskMark is a synthetic benchmarking app that tests data transfer rates under a variety of conditions. I've tested the six default tests, which measure performance in sequential and random workloads across different block sizes, queue depths, and thread counts. While this performance isn't exactly real world, it can still tell us the peak capabilities of a drive's performance.

MP700 Pro 2TB

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

SEQ1M Q8T1

12,389/11,666

12,391/11,701

11,682/9,537

10,073/10,197

SEQ1M Q1T1

9,255/9,610

9,297/9,636

9,169/9,216

8,601/9,622

SEQ128K Q32T1

12,296/11,467

12,281/11,479

11,471/9,563

9,778/10,151

RND4K Q32T16

6,390/6,839

6,382/7,031

5,697/6,536

6,106/6,797

RND4K Q32T1

1,114/828

1,169/858

1,178/851

1,155/839

RND4K Q1T1

100/392

101/393

101/397

100/385

Scores are organized by read/write and are measured in MB/s.

As expected, the MP700 Pro 2TB and Z540 2TB are neck and neck as they use the same NAND chips and controller. Meanwhile, the Gen5 12000 1TB is a fair bit behind in reads and especially writes in three of the workloads, which is typical for the 1TB versions of 12,000MB/s SSDs. All SSDs however are neck and neck in the lower thread count random tests, which don't particularly benefit from higher performance hardware.

Although the Z540 2TB was tested under the Z790 Taichi Lite's stock heatsink, it performed the same as the actively cooled MP700 Pro 2TB. This is because CrystalDiskMark just doesn't run long enough to saturate the heatsink with heat, so the Z540 2TB can maintain peak speed right alongside the MP700 Pro. For longer benchmarks, you'll see that this isn't the case.

3DMark

3DMark's storage benchmark runs real world workloads from popular (albeit aging) games like Overwatch, and assigns a score based on overall performance in data transfer and latency.

MP700 Pro 2TB

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

Score

5,593

5,783

5,772

5,620

The MP700 Pro 2TB is in 4th place, but it's by such a small margin that all SSDs here are effectively tied. Additionally, 3DMark's current storage benchmark is likely not suitable for testing PCIe 5.0 SSDs, as it predates DirectStorage and primarily tests loading times. I suspect 14,000MB/s SSDs won't do much better than 12,000MB/s drives like the MP700 Pro, as the MP700 Pro doesn't do much better than the FireCuda 540 2TB and other 10,000MB/s drives.

IOMeter

IOMeter is the final benchmark I have to show, and I use it to test how well an SSD can write over 15 minutes. When an SSD spends a long time writing data, two things happen. Firstly, its high-performance cache is eventually depleted, which can happen in less than a minute of writing. Secondly, the more an SSD is filled up, the weaker writing performance becomes, as there are fewer empty blocks to dump data into, forcing the SSD to spend resources reorganizing data. I've set up IOMeter to show the performance impact of these two important factors.

First, here's the long-term writing performance of the four SSDs when they're filled up 50%.

MP700 Pro 2TB

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

Average Write Speed

3,814

3,613

2,082

3,816

Scores are measured in MB/s.

The MP700 Pro 2TB is neck-and-neck with the FireCuda 540 2TB on average, while the Z540 2TB follows closely behind. The performance benefits of newer PCIe 5.0 NAND and controllers is diminished over the course of a 15-minute test and with the drives filled halfway, which is how the FireCuda 540 2TB can keep up so well. Meanwhile, the Gen5 12000 1TB is in dead last as it has fewer chips and consequently worse bandwidth.

This next graph shows how the performance of the MP700 Pro 2TB changes depending on how full it is. I tested it at 10% full, 50% full (the same data from the last test), and 90% full, and I also included the average speeds for the MP700 Pro 2TB alongside the other SSDs.

MP700 Pro 2TB

Z540 2TB

Gen5 12000 1TB

FireCuda 540 2TB

10% full

11,518

9,329

8,262

9,000

50% full

3,814

3,609

2,082

3,812

90% full

3,680

2,721

1,912

3,583

Scores are measured in MB/s.

At 10% full, the MP700 Pro 2TB is able to sustain a consistent write speed of nearly 12,000MB/s, well ahead of the other SSDs. However, that's down to cooling, which is a big factor in long, sustained writing workloads. If you use a cooler with the Z540 2TB, you can expect it to be just about as fast as the MP700 Pro 2TB, while the Gen5 12000 1TB gets a boost to roughly 9,400MB/s.

At the much higher fill rate of 90%, the Z540 2TB loses a significant amount of ground that puts the MP700 Pro 2TB firmly in first place, ahead of the FireCuda 540 2TB too. Although the Z540 2TB uses very similar hardware, it loses a significant amount of ground from the 50% full test to the 90% full test. This drive has great sustained writing performance and the typical 700TBW per terabyte endurance, so it's a great choice for anyone who finds themselves doing tons of writing operations.

Lastly, I want to discuss thermals, which are becoming increasingly important as PCIe 5.0 SSDs continue to run at high temperatures at peak performance. Without good cooling, an SSD has to thermal throttle, which means reduced reads and writes. The MP700 Pro's optional air cooler is interesting, as it doesn't use heatpipes or metal fins like the Gen5 12000's passive heatsink or Teamgroup's T-Force Dark Airflow I air-cooled heatsink. This allowed it to never thermal throttle and maintain peak writing performance of 11,500MB/s during the entire 15-minute-long test. The Z540 2TB on the other hand did thermal throttle under the Z790 Taichi Lite's stock heatsink. Under the T-Force Dark Airflow I however, the Z540 2TB was able to maintain over 11,000MB/s just like the MP700 Pro 2TB.

There was just one problem with the MP700 Pro's cooler: the cable. For some reason, Corsair decided to hook this fan up to a SATA power connector, which is a design choice I don't understand. SATA-powered fans always run at 100% and can't be controlled by software or firmware, and although the MP700 Pro's fan isn't very loud, it is high-pitched. If the fan just used a normal 4-pin plug, I would have called it the best PCIe 5.0 SSD cooler so far.

Should you buy the Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB?

You should buy the Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB if:

  • You want the fastest PCIe 5.0 SSD right now
  • You like the idea of liquid cooling an SSD
  • You have a large budget

You shouldn't buy the Corsair MP700 Pro 2TB if:

  • You want a PCIe 5.0 SSD with good bang for buck
  • You want a PCIe 5.0 SSD with a good overall cooling solution
  • You don't want to wait for next-generation PCIe 5.0 SSDs

Overall, the MP700 Pro 2TB is a great SSD. For enthusiasts with high-end PCs, the MP700 Pro 2TB is especially appealing as it's the only PCIe 5.0 SSD that can optionally come with a preinstalled waterblock.

Unfortunately, the MP700 Pro just doesn't do enough to really stand out and justify its price tag for the average PCIe 5.0 drive buyer. It performs pretty much the same as the Z540 while costing significantly more, and although an extra $40 isn't going to break the bank for someone in the market for a PCIe 5.0 SSD, it's still $40 that could be used for something else. item.title: Best MacBook external hard drives in 2023
item.itunes:title: Best MacBook external hard drives in 2023
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The best external drives, both solid-state (SSD) and hard-disk drives (HDD) provide creative professionals with a convenient and speedy solution for storing a large media library off of your MacBook Pro. While SSDs can provide you with the fastest file transfer experience, it is still worth looking into external HDDs as they can provide the highest capacity storage solutions for an affordable price. Whenever you are looking to buy a new portable drive, you'll want to focus on drive speed, connection type and transfer speed, storage capacity, and lastly, price. We've created a selection of our favorite external drives to use with the best MacBooks, and we've made sure to include drives that are great for a variety of creative and professional use cases.

  • SanDisk Extreme Pro V2 SSD
    Editor's choice
    $120 at Amazon
  • WD Black P50 Portable SSD

    Best value
